food_subgroup: Hard-boiled eggs in spiced Indian tomato-onion gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Indian Egg Curry. The nearest parent concept is a cooked egg dish in spiced sauce. A dedicated concept for egg-based Indian curries is recommended for future FoodOn extension.
ontology.foodex2_description: Hard-boiled eggs in spiced sauce, Indian style (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Indian egg curry)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A09EG is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for cooked egg dishes in sauce. No exact code for Indian-style egg curry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Indian egg curry. SNOMED CT food coverage for prepared ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.description: 1 bowl of egg curry (2 eggs + gravy)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of egg curry is approximately 250 g, typically comprising two hard-boiled eggs simmered in a medium-thick spiced onion-tomato gravy, served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EX000023
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database entry for Egg Curry (EX000023). Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Egg-Based Dishes — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; eggs have a GI of 0 and the composite dish GI is estimated at 30
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian Egg Curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Eggs have a published GI of 0 (University of Sydney GI Database). The composite dish GI of 30 is estimated based on the dominant protein-fat matrix of eggs (GI=0) combined with the low-carbohydrate onion-tomato gravy. Carbohydrate per 100g is 6.5g; GL per 250g serving = (30 × 16.25) / 100 = 4.9. Assessment type: estimated; confidence 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 318 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 795 mg sodium, representing about 35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit portion size to one serving (200–250 g) and request reduced salt when ordering, as the gravy and cooking salt concentrate sodium significantly; pairing with potassium-rich foods such as vegetables can help offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (6.5 g per 100 g) and low estimated glycemic index (GI ≈ 30); high protein (8.5 g per 100 g) from eggs supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg curry is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycaemic load (GL ≈ 4.9 per 250 g serving) and high-quality egg protein, which slows gastric emptying and reduces postprandial glucose excursions; monitor total meal carbohydrate when served with rice or bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content is 195 mg per 100 g (approximately 488 mg per 250 g serving), which exceeds the traditional 300 mg daily dietary cholesterol guideline; saturated fat is moderate at 2.5 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume egg curry in moderation — one serving (200–250 g) two to three times per week — as dietary cholesterol from eggs may modestly raise LDL in cholesterol-sensitive individuals, though the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, curcumin) and selenium content provide partial cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High selenium (22 mcg per 100 g) and vitamin D (1.5 mcg per 100 g) support immune modulation; turmeric provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Egg curry may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its selenium, vitamin D, and curcumin content, all of which contribute to immune regulation and reduction of inflammatory markers.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Curry (Anda Curry) is a widely enjoyed Pan-Indian dish in which hard-boiled eggs — often first shallow-fried in oil until golden and lightly crispy on the outside — are simmered in a medium-thick, fragrant onion-tomato gravy. The gravy is built by caramelising onions, adding pureed or finely chopped tomatoes, and blooming a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli powder, and garam masala. The result is a rich, mildly spiced, deeply savoury curry with a slightly tangy tomato base and a warming spice profile. Regional variations are significant: South Indian versions (Muttai Kulambu or Egg Roast) may incorporate coconut milk or tamarind for a tangier, creamier profile, while the Bengali Dim-er Dalna uses mustard oil and a more pungent spice base. Nutritionally, egg curry is an excellent source of high-quality complete protein (8.5 g per 100 g), vitamin B12 (0.85 mcg), riboflavin (0.22 mg), selenium (22 mcg), and vitamin D (1.5 mcg). The cholesterol content is notable at 195 mg per 100 g, reflecting the egg yolk contribution. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index (GI ≈ 30) and a low glycaemic load per serving (GL ≈ 4.9), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium is moderate to high at 318 mg per 100 g, primarily from cooking salt in the gravy. Egg curry is available at virtually all Indian and South Indian restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent, typically served as a main course with steamed rice, roti, or paratha.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and most micronutrients are sourced from the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database (verified, confidence 1.0). Three minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are from an enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9. The GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.7) as no direct GI measurement for Indian Egg Curry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is rated medium due to the estimated GI.
notes: 1. GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate based on the egg-dominant protein-fat matrix (eggs GI=0, University of Sydney GI Database) combined with the low-carbohydrate onion-tomato gravy; no direct GI measurement for Indian Egg Curry exists. 2. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (30 × 16.25) / 100 = 4.9. 3. Copper (0.08 mg), manganese (0.12 mg), and vitamin K (1.5 mcg) values are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 and should be verified against a primary source in a future revision. 4. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by ICMR-NIN for this entry; set to -1. 5. Water content not reported; set to -1. 6. Duplicate portion entries in the input (four identical '1 serving 250g' entries) have been deduplicated; canonical serving size set to 250 g. 7. The hypertension suitability assessment in the input was internally inconsistent (marked suitable=true but noted elevated sodium); the health_context entry has been corrected to concern_level=moderate with appropriate clinical guidance. 8. FoodEx2 code A09EG is a closest-parent match; no exact code exists for Indian egg curry in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
